does: This paper introduces an identifiable temporal causal representation learning framework for extracting interpretable features from large language models. The method extends sparse autoencoders (SAEs) by incorporating both time-delayed and instantaneous causal relations between features, addressing limitations of existing mechanistic interpretability approaches. The framework provides theoretical guarantees for identifiability—ensuring that discovered causal structures can be provably distinguished from alternatives—and is evaluated on synthetic datasets designed to match real-world complexity. The core contribution is combining causal representation learning principles with SAE-style feature extraction to make interpretability tools theoretically grounded while remaining computationally tractable for high-dimensional LLM concept spaces.
connects: This work builds on two established research directions. First, it extends mechanistic interpretability efforts that use sparse autoencoders to decompose LLM activations into interpretable features, which emerged as alternatives to black-box probing methods. Second, it incorporates insights from causal representation learning, a field focused on identifying latent causal structures in data with theoretical identifiability guarantees. The paper responds directly to recognized gaps in SAE-based approaches: their lack of temporal dependency modeling and absence of theoretical foundations for causality. It also addresses scalability challenges that have limited prior causal representation learning methods' applicability to high-dimensional spaces typical in language models.
enables: This framework opens several research directions in LLM interpretability. By providing theoretical guarantees for identified causal relations, it enables more principled analysis of feature interactions and mechanistic explanations of model behavior. The temporal-instantaneous representation could support better interventional analyses, helping researchers predict how causal edits to internal states affect model outputs. The identifiable structure creates foundations for downstream applications including improved model steering, detection of spurious learned features, and potentially more interpretable model training techniques. The combination of SAE efficiency with causal guarantees could also influence interpretability tool design more broadly, suggesting paths toward certified interpretability methods for other neural architectures and modalities.
